title: Evaluating higher-order product formulae for molecular ground-state energy estimation
authors: Hiromu Abe, Keita Kanno, Ryosuke Kimura, Masahiko Kamoshita, Kosuke Mitarai
abstract: We evaluate deterministic higher-order product formulae for molecular ground-state energy estimation. Motivated by recent fault-tolerant architectures in which non-Clifford operations may be generated more locally and cheaply than in conventional assumptions, we re-examine such formulae as practical candidates for quantum chemistry. Using one-dimensional hydrogen chains from $\mathrm{H}_2$ to $\mathrm{H}_{15}$ as benchmarks, we estimate both the total gate count and the depth of $R_Z$-rotation layers required to reach a target energy error. To make this comparison feasible at larger system sizes, we use a perturbative method to estimate the eigenvalue error induced by each product formula and thereby evaluate the cost of the corresponding phase-estimation procedure. Among the previously considered formulae, the eighth-order construction introduced by Morales et al. [M. E. S. Morales et al., "Greatly improved higher-order product formulae for quantum simulation," arXiv:2210.15817v2 (2024)] minimizes both cost metrics in the benchmark at a chemically relevant target error. We also find that increasing the formal order does not automatically reduce the total cost: near chemical accuracy, the tenth-order formula introduced in the same work can be less efficient than the eighth-order one. Motivated by this observation, we construct a new fourth-order formula; it achieves the lowest total gate count among the formulae considered for all H-chain instances near chemical accuracy and over much of the 0.1-10 mHa target-error window for most instances, while also reducing the $R_Z$-layer depth. These results clarify how deterministic higher-order product formulae should be selected for molecular ground-state energy estimation.
summary: This paper rigorously compares the resource costs (gate count and circuit depth) of different higher-order product formulae for simulating molecular ground-state energies. Using hydrogen chains as benchmarks, the authors demonstrate that the optimal formula depends on the required accuracy, proposing a new fourth-order method that achieves superior performance over existing state-of-the-art formulae near chemical accuracy.
main_problem: The paper evaluates and compares the computational cost of various deterministic higher-order product formulae used to estimate the ground-state energy of molecules via quantum simulation.
main_result: A newly constructed fourth-order product formula was shown to outperform established eighth-order formulae in minimizing both the total gate count and the RZ-layer depth near chemical accuracy.
method: The cost is evaluated by estimating the total gate count and the depth of RZ-rotation layers required to achieve a target energy error ($\epsilon_E$) using perturbative analysis.
model_or_system: The benchmarks use one-dimensional hydrogen chains ($	ext{H}_2$ to $	ext{H}_{15}$) modeled by a Hamiltonian approximated via product formulae for the time-evolution operator.
key_observables: Total gate count ($F$), depth of RZ-rotation layers ($D_{	ext{RZ}}$), and the energy error ($\epsilon_E$).
important_parameters: Target energy error ($\epsilon_E = 1.6 	imes 10^{-4} 	ext{ Ha}$), formal order of the product formula ($p$), and system size ($N$).
main_assumptions: The analysis uses a perturbative method to estimate eigenvalue error, and the layer depth estimate neglects the depth contribution from the Clifford basis-change circuit.
figures_summary: Figures compare the total gate count and RZ-layer depth for different formulae across H-chain sizes, and show the scaling relationships between these costs and system size.
paper_structure: The paper first analyzes the phase estimation error scaling, then details the cost metrics (gate count and layer depth) derived from product formulae approximations, compares various orders (2nd, 4th, 8th, 10th), and concludes by proposing and validating a new optimized fourth-order formula.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical resource estimation for quantum chemistry simulations, providing concrete guidelines on selecting the most efficient quantum circuit decomposition method for molecular energy calculations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. For H-chains at the target energy error εE = 1.6 × 10−4 Ha, we plot the total gate count F (number of Pauli rotations) as dots for each qubit count. The colored straight lines show the fitted scaling on logarithmic scales for both axes. Legend entries are, in order, second order (green), conventional fourth order (cyan; labeled “4th” in the figure), eighth order by Yoshida (orange), eighth order by Morales et al. (magenta), and tenth order by Morales et al. (greenyel- low).

FIG. 2. For H-chains at the target energy error εE = 1.6 × 10−4 Ha, we plot the depth of RZ-rotation layers as dots for each qubit count. The colored straight lines show the fitted scaling on logarithmic scales for both axes. Legend entries are, in order, second order (green), conventional fourth order (cyan; labeled “4th” in the figure), eighth order by Yoshida (orange), eighth order by Morales et al. (magenta), and tenth order by Morales et al. (greenyellow).

FIG. 3. As in Fig. 1, we plot the total gate count F at the target energy error εE = 1.6 × 10−4 Ha together with the fit- ted scaling on log–log axes. We compare the eighth-order product formula by Morales et al. (magenta, “8th (Morales et al.)”) with our newly constructed fourth-order product formula (blue, “4th (ours)”). The dashed curve shows the ab- solute difference between the two estimated total gate counts, plotted on the right axis.

FIG. 4. As in Fig. 2, we plot the depth of RZ-rotation lay- ers at the target energy error εE = 1.6 × 10−4 Ha together with the fitted scaling on log–log axes. We compare our new fourth-order product formula (blue, “4th (ours)”) with the eighth-order product formula by Morales et al. (magenta, “8th (Morales et al.)”). The dashed curve shows the absolute difference between the two estimated depths of RZ-rotation layers, plotted on the right axis.
FIG. 5. Values of α obtained by fitting with p fixed to the order of each product formula for the H-chain instances con- sidered in this work.
